---
title: "Seeing the Sampling Distribution by Simulation"
date: "2026-06-22"
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
---

# Seeing the Sampling Distribution by Simulation {.title}

::: notes
Welcome back to Estimators and Their Sampling Distributions, part six of seven. We have talked about the distribution of x bar across all possible samples as if it were a real object. It is, and it has a name. In this part we name it, then fake the universe on a computer to see it directly.
:::

## Naming the Object

- **Sampling distribution:** the distribution of estimates
- across all possible samples of size $N$ from the population
- So far we have only two *summaries* of it
- Center: $\mathbb{E}[\bar{x}]=\mu$
- Spread: $\operatorname{Var}(\bar{x})=\sigma^2/N$

::: notes
The sampling distribution of an estimator is the probability distribution of the estimates you would get across all possible samples of a given size N from the population. It is the object tying this whole lesson together. Up to now we have computed only two summaries of it — its center, the expected value of x bar, which is mu, and its spread, the variance of x bar, which is sigma squared over N. But the full distribution has a shape, and the cleanest way to see a shape you cannot compute by hand is to fake the universe on a computer and just look at it.
:::

## We Are Gods in a Simulation

- Real life: Priya draws *one* sample, gets *one* estimate
- She can never see the whole distribution — one draw only
- In simulation we know the true population
- Draw 200 claims, compute $\bar{x}$, record it, repeat 10,000 times
- The 10,000 values *are* a picture of the distribution

::: notes
Here is the trick, and it is the conceptual core of the entire lesson, so slow down for it. In real life Priya gets one sample of two hundred claims and therefore one estimate, x bar equals eight thousand four hundred ten. She can never see the sampling distribution, because she only ever draws once. But in a simulation we are gods: we know the true population, so we can draw a fresh sample of two hundred, compute its x bar, write it down, throw the sample away, and repeat ten thousand times. The ten thousand x bar values we collect are a picture of the sampling distribution. We are watching the random variable x bar do its thing, over and over, instead of seeing it just once.
:::

## Building the Population

- Model claims with a **lognormal** — right-skewed losses
- Many small claims, a thin tail of catastrophes
- Calibrated so the population mean is $\mu=\$8{,}000$
- Draw one sample of 200 — Priya's reality
- Then 10,000 alternate universes, each its own 200 claims

::: notes
Let us build it. We model individual claims with a right-skewed distribution — a lognormal, the standard rough model for insurance losses, with many small claims and a thin tail of catastrophes — calibrated so the population mean is exactly eight thousand dollars. The code draws one sample of two hundred, like Priya's reality, and prints her single estimate. Then it defines a sampling-distribution routine: draw two hundred claims, take the mean, and repeat ten thousand times, collecting ten thousand x bar values. Each repetition is one alternate universe with its own two hundred claims. We are manufacturing, by brute force, the distribution Priya can only ever sample once.
:::

## Two Lines That Earn an Ovation

- Mean of the 10,000 $\bar{x}$ values lands on \$8,000
- Centered on $\mu$ — unbiasedness, now *seen*
- Std dev of the estimates matches $\sigma/\sqrt{N}$
- That spread has a name: the **standard error**
- Formula and histogram are two views of one object

::: notes
Two lines of that output deserve a standing ovation. First, the mean of the ten thousand x bar values lands essentially on eight thousand dollars: the sampling distribution is centered on mu, exactly as the unbiasedness proof promised — now seen, not just derived. Second, the standard deviation of those ten thousand estimates matches sigma over the square root of N from part three. That standard deviation of the sampling distribution has its own famous name — the standard error — and the simulation shows you literally what it measures: the typical sample-to-sample wobble of your estimate. The formula and the histogram are two views of the same object. The algebra we did earlier and the picture on the screen are describing one and the same thing.
:::

## Key Takeaway {.title}

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The **sampling distribution** is the distribution of estimates across all possible samples of size $N$. Real life gives one draw; simulation lets us draw 10,000 and *see* it. The 10,000 means center on $\mu$ and spread by $\sigma/\sqrt{N}$ — unbiasedness and the standard error, made visible. Next: how the *shape* changes with $N$.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part six: the sampling distribution is the distribution of an estimator's estimates across all possible samples of size N. We computed only its center and spread, so to see its full shape we faked the universe — drawing ten thousand samples of two hundred and collecting their means. Those ten thousand values center on eight thousand dollars, confirming unbiasedness, and spread by sigma over root N, which is the standard error, made visible. In part seven we look at the one thing the formulas cannot give us — the shape of the histogram, and how it transforms as the sample size grows.
:::
